Module 2
Exploring AI Use Cases and Applications
Module Overview
A
AI Fundamentals Recap
AI, ML, DL, Generative AI — definitions and content types
B
Industry Use Cases
Media, Retail, Healthcare, Life Sciences, Finance, Manufacturing
C
Core AI Applications
Computer Vision, NLP, IDP, Fraud Detection
D
When to Use ML
Appropriate vs. inappropriate scenarios, decision criteria

Supervised, Unsupervised, Reinforcement — subtypes and use cases
F

Adaptability, creativity, personalization, scalability

Risks, hallucinations, bias, mitigation strategies

Model Selection
Factors, FM examples, cost vs. performance trade-offs
I
Business Metrics
Success measurement: satisfaction, ARPU, conversion, ROI
Prerequisites
Module 1 — Fundamentals of ML & AI
Leads To
AWS service selection, model deployment, business alignment
Module Introduction

Module 1 established the vocabulary — what AI, ML, Deep Learning, and Generative AI are. Module 2 asks the next question: where does it actually go? This module bridges theory to practice by mapping AI capabilities onto real industries, defining the four core application domains, and building a framework for deciding when ML is the right tool and which technique fits a given problem.

It also introduces the practical considerations that determine whether an AI solution succeeds in production — model selection trade-offs, Generative AI's unique risks, mitigation strategies, and the business metrics used to measure real-world impact. By the end of this module, you will be able to connect a business problem to the correct AI approach and evaluate it against cost, performance, and compliance constraints.

A
AI Fundamentals Recap
A
A1The Four-Layer Hierarchy

The four core terms from Module 1 reappear throughout this module as anchors. Artificial Intelligence is the umbrella field — any system that mimics human intelligence. Machine Learning learns patterns from data instead of following explicit rules. Deep Learning uses layered neural networks to handle complex, unstructured tasks. Generative AI, built on Deep Learning, goes beyond prediction to create entirely new content.

The Persistent Distinction

Generative AI creates — it produces novel outputs (text, images, audio, code, video) that did not previously exist. Traditional ML predicts or analyzes — it classifies, scores, or extracts from existing data. This distinction recurs across every industry use case in this module.

A2Generative AI Content Types

Generative AI is not limited to one output medium. Understanding what it can generate is prerequisite to mapping it to industry applications.

Text
Conversations, summaries, articles, reports, translations, code documentation
Visual (Image/Video)
Images, illustrations, video scenes, visual design assets, product renders
Audio
Music generation, speech synthesis, voiceovers, sound effects
Code
Programming outputs, function generation, code review, debugging assistance
Key Points
  • AI hierarchy: AI ⊃ ML ⊃ DL ⊃ Generative AI — same nesting as Module 1.
  • Generative AI creates; traditional ML predicts or analyzes. Never conflate them.
  • Four output types: Text · Visual · Audio · Code — each maps to different industry use cases.
B
Real-World AI Use Cases Across Industries
B
B1Why Industries Adopt AI

AI creates business value through three primary mechanisms: automation (replacing repetitive manual work), optimization (improving decisions or processes beyond human capacity), and personalization (tailoring experiences at a scale that rule-based systems cannot achieve). These mechanisms appear differently depending on the industry, but the underlying logic is always the same — AI handles what rules cannot scale to. [+]

B2Industry-by-Industry Breakdown
Media & Entertainment
  • Content Generation — scripts, stories, auto-summaries
  • Virtual Reality — immersive AI-generated environments
  • News Generation — automatic article creation and summarization
Retail
  • Review Summaries — condensed product insight from thousands of reviews
  • Pricing Optimization — dynamic margin maximization
  • Virtual Try-ons — simulate product fitting without physical contact
  • Store Layout Optimization — improve shopping flow and conversion
Healthcare
  • Clinical Documentation (HealthScribe) — auto-generate clinical notes from provider-patient conversations
  • Personalized Medicine — tailor treatments to individual patient profiles
  • Medical Imaging Enhancement — improve diagnostic accuracy from scans
Life Sciences
  • Drug Discovery — generate and evaluate molecular structures
  • Protein Folding Prediction — predict 3D protein structures (e.g., AlphaFold) [~]
  • Synthetic Biology — engineer biological systems computationally
Financial Services
  • Fraud Detection — identify anomalous transactions in real time
  • Portfolio Management — simulate market scenarios for risk assessment
  • Debt Collection Optimization — improve strategy and timing
Manufacturing
  • Predictive Maintenance — detect failure before it occurs to reduce downtime
  • Process Optimization — improve throughput and efficiency
  • Product Design Generation — generate designs within constraint sets
  • Material Science — discover new materials computationally
AWS HealthScribe

AWS HealthScribe is specifically called out in the source material as the AWS service for clinical documentation — it automatically generates clinical notes from provider-patient conversations using AI, reducing administrative burden on healthcare providers.

Key Points
  • AI creates value through automation, optimization, and personalization — these three mechanisms recur across all industries.
  • Six industries: Media · Retail · Healthcare · Life Sciences · Financial Services · Manufacturing. Know at least two use cases per industry.
  • AWS HealthScribe = clinical documentation via AI — a named AWS service to remember.
C
Core AI Application Domains
C
C1Computer Vision

Computer Vision enables machines to interpret and understand visual information — images and video — the way humans do. It is one of the oldest and most mature subfields of AI, now accelerated significantly by deep learning architectures. [+]

Core Tasks
  • Image Classification — assign a label to an image ("cat," "dog," "tumor")
  • Object Detection — identify and locate multiple objects within an image with bounding boxes
  • Segmentation — classify every pixel in an image to its object (most granular level)
Industry Use Cases
  • Autonomous Driving — detect lanes, pedestrians, signs in real time
  • Healthcare Imaging — improve accuracy of radiology and pathology diagnoses
  • Public Safety — facial recognition for surveillance and access control
Key Points
  • Three CV tasks in order of granularity: Classification (whole image) → Detection (located objects) → Segmentation (every pixel).
  • AWS service: Amazon Rekognition handles image/video analysis for all three levels.
C2Natural Language Processing (NLP)

Natural Language Processing (NLP) enables machines to understand, interpret, and generate human language. NLP powers everything from spell-checkers to large language models — it is the discipline connecting raw text to machine comprehension.

Core Tasks
  • Text Classification — categorize documents by topic or intent
  • Sentiment Analysis — detect positive, negative, or neutral tone
  • Translation — convert text between languages while preserving meaning
  • Text Generation — produce coherent language continuations or responses
Industry Use Cases
  • Insurance — extract key clauses and data from policy documents
  • Telecom — generate personalized product recommendations from usage patterns
  • Education — power chatbot Q&A systems for student support
Key Points
  • Four NLP tasks: Text Classification · Sentiment Analysis · Translation · Text Generation.
  • AWS services: Amazon Comprehend (NLP insights), Amazon Translate (translation), Amazon Lex (chatbots using NLU).
C3Intelligent Document Processing (IDP)

Intelligent Document Processing (IDP) applies AI to extract structured information and insights from unstructured or semi-structured documents — PDFs, scanned forms, contracts, invoices, and records that cannot be processed by simple rule-based parsing.

Core Functions
  • Data Extraction — pull specific values from forms, tables, and text
  • Classification — route documents to the correct workflow by type
  • Summarization — condense long documents into key points
Industry Use Cases
  • Finance — automate loan application processing from submitted forms
  • Legal — automate contract review and clause extraction
  • Healthcare — process insurance claims and patient records
IDP vs. NLP

IDP is a superset workflow that applies NLP (and CV for scanned images) to document-specific problems. NLP is a capability; IDP is an application of that capability to document-centric business processes. [+] AWS service: Amazon Textract for OCR and form extraction.

C4Fraud Detection

Fraud Detection uses ML models to identify anomalous or malicious patterns in transaction data, user behavior, or network activity. Unlike rule-based fraud filters (which require explicit rules for every known fraud type), ML-based fraud detection learns what normal looks like and flags statistical deviations — catching novel fraud patterns that no rule anticipated. [+]

Finance
Anti-money laundering (AML), transaction anomaly detection, credit card fraud
Retail
Account takeover protection, return fraud, fake review detection
Telecom
SIM card fraud, subscription abuse, network intrusion monitoring
Key Points
  • Four core application domains: Computer Vision · NLP · IDP · Fraud Detection.
  • Each domain = a category of AI capability, not a single algorithm or service.
  • Fraud Detection is ML-based — it learns normal patterns and flags deviations, unlike rigid rule-based filters.
D
When to Use ML — and When Not To
D
D1The Core Decision Framework

ML is a powerful tool, but it is not always the right one. The decision to use ML versus a deterministic (rule-based) approach depends on the complexity and scale of the problem. If a human expert can write down all the rules that solve a problem — and those rules will hold at any scale — ML adds cost without benefit. ML becomes necessary when rules are too complex to enumerate or when the problem must scale beyond what rules can handle.

USE
Complex Rules — too many edge cases to define manually
Example: Spam filtering — thousands of evolving patterns, linguistic tricks, and sender behaviors that no static ruleset can capture.
USE
High Variability — many interacting factors influence the outcome
Example: Credit risk scoring — hundreds of variables interact non-linearly in ways rules cannot express.
USE
Large Scale — massive datasets that humans cannot manually process
Example: Real-time recommendation systems serving millions of users simultaneously.
SKIP
Simple Logic — clear deterministic rules already exist
Example: Calculating tax — a formula applies universally. ML adds complexity with no benefit.
SKIP
Low Complexity — predefined steps are sufficient
Example: Converting Celsius to Fahrenheit. No pattern to learn — the rule is complete.
Exam Framing

When a question describes a scenario with complex, evolving patterns or massive data scale — choose ML. When the problem has a known, deterministic solution — rule-based logic is more appropriate. ML is not universally better; it is specifically better when rules cannot scale or be defined.

Key Points
  • Use ML for: complex rules, high variability, large scale.
  • Skip ML for: simple deterministic logic, low-complexity tasks with known rule sets.
  • Canonical example for ML: spam filtering. Canonical example against ML: basic arithmetic.
E
ML Techniques and Their Use Cases
E
E1Supervised Learning — Subtypes and Applications

Supervised Learning trains on labeled data — every example has a known correct output — and learns to predict outputs for new, unseen inputs. It is the most widely deployed paradigm in production ML. Two subtypes determine what kind of output the model predicts.

Classification
  • Predicts a categorical output — one of a finite set of classes
  • Fraud detection — fraud / not fraud
  • Image classification — cat / dog / bird
  • Customer churn prediction — will churn / will not churn
  • Medical diagnostics — disease positive / negative
Regression
  • Predicts a continuous output — a numeric value along a range
  • Weather forecasting — predict tomorrow's temperature
  • Market trend prediction — predict stock price movement
  • Life expectancy estimation — predict years remaining
  • Population growth modeling — predict future headcount
Classification vs. Regression — Quick Test

Ask: "Is the answer one of a fixed set of categories, or is it a number on a continuous scale?" Categories → Classification. Continuous number → Regression. "Will this customer buy?" = Classification. "How much will this customer spend?" = Regression. [+]

Key Points
  • Supervised = labeled data → predict output. Input requires a known correct answer for every training example.
  • Classification = categorical output (discrete classes). Regression = continuous output (numeric value).
  • Know at least two use cases for each subtype.
E2Unsupervised Learning — Subtypes and Applications

Unsupervised Learning discovers hidden structure in data without any labeled examples. There is no correct answer provided — the model creates its own organization of the data. This is valuable when labeling is expensive, impossible, or unnecessary — or when the goal is exploration rather than prediction.

Clustering
  • Groups similar data points together without predefined categories
  • Customer segmentation — group users by behavior profile
  • Targeted marketing — identify audience clusters for campaigns
  • Recommendation systems — group users with similar preferences
Dimensionality Reduction
  • Reduces the number of input features while preserving important structure
  • Data visualization — project high-dimensional data to 2D/3D
  • Compression — reduce data size for storage or transmission
  • Feature extraction — identify the most meaningful variables in large datasets
Key Points
  • Unsupervised = no labels — the model discovers structure on its own.
  • Clustering = group similar items (customer segmentation). Dimensionality Reduction = simplify feature space (visualization, compression).
  • Critical distinction: unsupervised learning is not "wrong" — it simply has no ground truth to check against.
E3Reinforcement Learning

Reinforcement Learning (RL) is fundamentally different from both supervised and unsupervised paradigms. An agent learns by interacting with an environment — taking actions, receiving reward signals for correct actions and penalty signals for incorrect ones. Over many iterations, the agent develops a policy — a strategy for choosing actions that maximizes cumulative reward.

There is no dataset of correct answers. The agent discovers what works through trial and error. RL is particularly powerful for sequential decision-making problems where the optimal action at each step depends on long-term consequences. [+]

AWS DeepRacer

AWS DeepRacer is an autonomous 1/18th-scale race car that learns to navigate a track using reinforcement learning. The agent (car) receives rewards for staying on track and progressing toward the finish, and penalties for going off-track. It is the canonical hands-on RL example in the AWS ecosystem.

Key Points
  • RL components: Agent (decision-maker) · Environment (interaction space) · Reward/Penalty (feedback signal) · Policy (learned strategy).
  • RL learns through trial and error — no labeled dataset, no predefined correct answers.
  • AWS example: DeepRacer (autonomous driving via reward-based learning).
  • RL is best for sequential decision problems — game playing, robotics, resource scheduling. [+]
F
Capabilities of Generative AI
F
F1The Seven Capabilities

Generative AI is not a single capability — it is a cluster of properties that collectively explain why it creates so much business value across different domains. Understanding each capability helps you match Generative AI to the right problem.

Adaptability
A single FM can flex across completely different tasks and domains without being rebuilt for each one. The same model that writes code can also summarize legal documents.
Responsiveness
Generates output in real time — conversational exchange, instant drafts, live translation — without pre-computing or batch delays.
Simplicity
Automates workflows that previously required specialized technical skill. A non-developer can generate working code by describing intent in plain language.
Creativity
Produces genuinely novel outputs — new designs, original text, unexplored solutions — not just recombinations of existing templates.
Data Efficiency
Foundation Models pretrained on large datasets can be adapted to specific tasks with relatively small amounts of new data, compared to training from scratch.
Personalization
Tailors outputs to individual users at scale — personalized marketing copy, custom learning plans, user-specific recommendations — that rule-based systems cannot achieve per-user.
Scalability
Produces content at a volume and speed impossible for human teams. A single model can generate thousands of product descriptions, emails, or code modules per minute.
Key Points
  • Seven capabilities: Adaptability · Responsiveness · Simplicity · Creativity · Data Efficiency · Personalization · Scalability.
  • The core business value proposition: Generative AI enables automation and creativity at scale — simultaneously. Traditional automation lacked creativity; human creativity lacked scale.
G
Challenges and Risks of Generative AI
G
G1Key Risks

Generative AI's power introduces risks that do not exist in traditional deterministic software. These risks arise from the probabilistic, learned nature of AI outputs — outputs are not guaranteed to be correct, safe, or compliant. Understanding each risk is necessary for responsible deployment.

Hallucinations
The model generates plausible but factually false information with apparent confidence. The most critical and exam-tested risk. The model has no concept of "truth" — it predicts likely text, not correct text.
Toxicity
Offensive, harmful, discriminatory, or inappropriate outputs produced from biased training data or adversarial prompts.
Privacy Concerns
Models may memorize and regenerate personal data seen during training, or process confidential information in ways that violate data governance requirements.
Regulatory Violations
AI outputs may expose organizations to legal risk by revealing sensitive regulated data (PII, PHI, financial records) or by generating non-compliant content.
Social Risks
Reputationally harmful content, deepfakes, misinformation, or outputs that damage brand integrity or public trust.
Interpretability
It is often difficult to explain why a model produced a specific output — making audit, debugging, and regulatory compliance challenging.
Nondeterminism
The same input can produce different outputs across runs. This inconsistency is problematic for high-stakes applications requiring predictable, reproducible responses.
Hallucination — Exam Priority

Hallucinations are the most frequently tested Gen AI risk. The definition is precise: hallucinations are outputs that are plausible-sounding but factually incorrect. The model does not "know" it is wrong — it generates the most statistically likely continuation of the prompt, regardless of factual accuracy. This is why AI outputs must always be verified before use in high-stakes contexts.

G2Mitigation Strategies

Each risk class has corresponding mitigation strategies. These are not eliminations — they are controls that reduce the probability and impact of harmful outputs.

Technical Controls
  • Guardrails — filter or block harmful output categories before they reach the user
  • Verification / RAG — ground model outputs in retrieved factual sources to reduce hallucination
  • Model Testing — evaluate outputs for bias, toxicity, and accuracy before deployment
  • Monitoring — continuously detect inconsistencies and drift in production
  • Security Measures — encryption, firewalls, access controls for data protection
Data Controls
  • Data Anonymization — remove or mask personally identifiable information before training or prompting
  • Data Curation — remove harmful, biased, or low-quality content from training data
Key Points
  • Seven risks: Hallucinations · Toxicity · Privacy · Regulatory Violations · Social Risks · Interpretability · Nondeterminism.
  • Hallucination = plausible but false. The model predicts likely text, not correct text.
  • Primary mitigation for hallucination: Guardrails + Verification (RAG grounds outputs in facts).
  • AI outputs must always be verified before use in high-stakes contexts.
H
Model Selection Factors
H
H1The Five Core Selection Factors

Choosing the right model for a production system is not just a technical decision — it is a business decision. The wrong model can be accurate but too slow, too expensive, or non-compliant. Good model selection balances five factors simultaneously.

Model Type
Does the model support the specific task? A text-generation model cannot produce images. A vision model cannot translate text. Task-capability alignment is the first gate.
Performance
Accuracy and reliability on the target task. Measured by benchmarks, human evaluation, or task-specific metrics. Higher performance usually means higher cost.
Constraints
Available compute resources (GPU/CPU/memory), data availability, and deployment environment (cloud vs. on-premises vs. edge).
Compliance
Does the model meet data privacy regulations (GDPR, HIPAA), ethical guidelines, and organizational policies? Non-compliant models are disqualified regardless of performance.
Cost
Larger, more capable models cost more per inference. Smaller, faster models reduce cost but may sacrifice quality. The right answer depends on the use case tolerance for each.
H2Foundation Model Examples

Different Foundation Models have different strengths. Knowing which FM is suited to which task type is directly tested in AWS certification exams.

AI21 Jurassic-2
Text generation, summarization, Q&A — strong on long-form text tasks
Amazon Titan
Embeddings, semantic search, Q&A — AWS-native FM family via Bedrock
Anthropic Claude
Chat, reasoning, coding, analysis — known for safety alignment and long context
Stable Diffusion
Image generation from text prompts — leading open-source diffusion model
Cohere Command
NLP tasks — classification, summarization, enterprise text processing
Meta Llama
General text processing — open-weight model family, widely used for fine-tuning
H3Cost vs. Performance Trade-off

Model size directly affects both capability and cost. There is no universally "best" model — the optimal choice is always relative to the use case's requirements and budget.

Large Models
  • Higher accuracy and reasoning depth
  • Handle complex, nuanced tasks
  • Higher cost per inference
  • Slower response time at the same hardware
  • Use when: quality is critical, budget allows
Small Models
  • Lower cost per inference
  • Faster response — better latency
  • Lower resource requirements
  • Trade-off: reduced accuracy on complex tasks
  • Use when: speed and cost dominate, task is well-defined
Key Points
  • Five selection factors: Model Type · Performance · Constraints · Compliance · Cost.
  • Know six FM examples: Jurassic-2 (text) · Titan (embeddings) · Claude (chat/reasoning) · Stable Diffusion (images) · Cohere (NLP) · Llama (general text).
  • Model selection = task + cost + performance balance. No single winner — context decides.
  • Compliance is a hard gate — a non-compliant model is disqualified regardless of accuracy.
I
Business Metrics for Generative AI
I
I1Measuring Real-World AI Success

A model can achieve excellent technical performance — high accuracy, low latency — and still fail to create business value. Conversely, a moderate-accuracy model deployed in the right context can transform a business operation. Technical metrics tell you how well the model performs. Business metrics tell you whether it matters. [+]

Both dimensions are required. AWS exams test the ability to evaluate AI solutions not just on accuracy but on whether they align with and advance business objectives.

User Satisfaction
Measured through surveys, ratings, or qualitative feedback. Captures whether users find the AI output helpful, accurate, and trustworthy.
ARPU
Average Revenue Per User — tracks whether AI-powered personalization or recommendations translates to increased spending per customer.
Cross-Domain Performance
Measures how effectively a model generalizes across multiple domains or use cases — relevant for evaluating FM versatility in multi-purpose deployments.
Conversion Rate
The percentage of users who complete a desired action (purchase, sign-up, click). A direct indicator of whether AI-driven personalization or recommendations change behavior.
Efficiency
Resource and time optimization — how much faster or cheaper a process runs with AI versus without. Captures operational ROI.
Metrics are Use-Case Specific

No metric is universally applicable. A customer service chatbot prioritizes User Satisfaction and resolution time. An e-commerce recommendation engine prioritizes Conversion Rate and ARPU. A content moderation tool prioritizes accuracy and regulatory compliance. The choice of business metric must follow the business objective. [+]

Key Points
  • Five business metrics: User Satisfaction · ARPU · Cross-Domain Performance · Conversion Rate · Efficiency.
  • Technical performance alone is insufficient — AI success requires business metric alignment.
  • Metrics must be chosen based on the specific business objective — there is no universal success metric.
  • Core insight: AI success = technical performance + business value. Both halves are required.
Module Summary
  • Generative AI creates new content (text, visual, audio, code); traditional ML analyzes or predicts from existing data. Six industries — Media, Retail, Healthcare, Life Sciences, Finance, Manufacturing — deploy AI through automation, optimization, and personalization.
  • Four core application domains: Computer Vision (classify, detect, segment) · NLP (classify, analyze, translate, generate) · IDP (extract, classify, summarize documents) · Fraud Detection (anomaly identification from behavioral patterns).
  • Use ML when rules are too complex or scale is too high. Skip ML when a deterministic rule-based solution already exists. Canonical ML case: spam filtering. Non-ML case: basic calculation.
  • Supervised learning (labeled data) splits into Classification (categorical output) and Regression (continuous output). Unsupervised learning (no labels) splits into Clustering (group similar items) and Dimensionality Reduction (simplify feature space). Reinforcement learning learns via Agent → Environment → Reward/Penalty → Policy.
  • Generative AI has seven capabilities (Adaptability, Responsiveness, Simplicity, Creativity, Data Efficiency, Personalization, Scalability) and seven risks (Hallucinations, Toxicity, Privacy, Regulatory Violations, Social Risks, Interpretability, Nondeterminism). Hallucination — plausible but false output — is the primary exam-tested risk. Model selection balances task type, performance, constraints, compliance, and cost. AI success requires both technical performance and business metric alignment.
